title: SpaceX is gearing up for Starship's 13th test flight later this week
gist: SpaceX's 13th Starship test flight will carry 20 functioning Starlink V3 satellites and retry a Raptor in-space engine relight and Super Heavy booster splashdown that both failed on Flight 12, with a successful flight positioning Starship for its first orbital launch and eventual 60 Tbps-per-launch Starlink V3 deployment capacity.

themes.theme: Starship Flight 13 objectives and Starlink V3 scaling

insights.claim: Starship's 13th test flight will carry 20 functioning Starlink V3 broadband satellites in its cargo deployer (replacing prior mass simulators), and will attempt to briefly establish laser communication links between the V3s and other low-Earth-orbit spacecraft to validate V3's interoperability with SpaceX's previous Starlink generation.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: First live-payload test of Starlink V3 satellites on Starship is a concrete near-term milestone for SpaceX's next-gen broadband capacity rollout; successful interoperability validation de-risks the eventual mass deployment phase.

insights.claim: A fully loaded Starship will be able to launch up to 60 Starlink V3 satellites per flight, adding 60 Tbps of network capacity per launch, compared to about 2.6 Tbps added per Falcon 9 launch carrying Starlink V2 satellites — roughly a 23x capacity increase per launch.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Quantifies the step-change economics Starship unlocks for Starlink network capacity expansion; once operational, this dramatically lowers SpaceX's cost-per-Tbps of deployed broadband capacity versus the current Falcon 9-based rollout, strengthening Starlink's competitive moat versus rivals like Amazon's Kuiper and OneWeb.
insights.locator: Fully loaded Starships will be capable of launching up to 60 Starlink V3s

insights.claim: On Starship's prior flight (Flight 12) in May, the ship skipped a planned in-space Raptor engine relight test after one Raptor engine shut down prematurely during launch, and the Super Heavy booster lost control after stage separation—its directional flip was off by about 90 degrees—causing five of its 33 engines to fail to relight for the boostback burn, ending that burn early and preventing the planned water landing.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Identifies the two specific unresolved technical failure points (Raptor in-space relight and booster stage-separation/relight reliability) that Flight 13 must clear before SpaceX can attempt an orbital Starship launch — a clear checklist for tracking program progress and risk.
insights.quote: At stage separation on Flight 12, slight differences in engine startup on the ship caused the directional flip of the booster to be off by approximately 90 degrees.

insights.claim: SpaceX says it has made hardware and operational modifications to the Raptor engine and Super Heavy booster (including engine alarm/abort updates) to address the interconnected causes of the Flight 12 relight failures, and Flight 13's plan includes retrying both the Raptor in-space relight and the booster's boostback/splashdown.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: A near-perfect Flight 13 would put SpaceX on the cusp of attempting an orbital Starship launch, unlocking subsequent milestones (satellite deployment, in-orbit refueling demos, booster reuse at Starbase) that are prerequisites for both Starlink V3 mass deployment and NASA's Artemis Moon program.
insights.locator: Several hardware and operational modifications have been made

insights.claim: Elon Musk said in a February interview on the Dwarkesh Podcast that the single biggest remaining problem for Starship is making the heat shield reusable, noting that no one has ever made a reusable orbital heat shield, and that although Starship has completed soft ocean landings, it has lost many heat shield tiles each time, meaning extensive refurbishment would currently be required before reflight.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Musk himself frames heat shield reusability — not propulsion or structure — as the critical bottleneck to Starship's core value proposition of rapid, low-cost reusability; investors and NASA program planners should treat heat-shield tile durability data (being gathered via 'load sensing tiles' on Flight 13) as the key leading indicator of program timeline risk.
insights.quote: What's the single biggest remaining problem for Starship? It's having the heat shield be reusable,
insights.locator: quote from Elon Musk on the Dwarkesh Podcast

insights.claim: On Flight 13, SpaceX will test modified heat shield tiles and attachment mechanisms along with new 'load sensing tiles' that measure stress during a higher dynamic-pressure ascent profile than previous flights, a change made in exchange for increased payload-to-orbit capacity.
insights.speaker: Stephen Clark
insights.so_what: Shows SpaceX is deliberately trading structural margin (higher dynamic pressure/stress) for payload capacity gains, a design choice that raises near-term technical risk on this flight but targets the ultimate goal of higher revenue-generating payload mass per launch.
insights.locator: The shield will also have 'load sensing tiles'
